planting.note: Divide rhizomatous clumps in spring or plant container-grown plants in moist to moderately dry, humus-rich soil in full to part shade; jade lily tolerates dry shade under trees better than most rhizomatous groundcovers and is semi-evergreen to fully evergreen in zones 7–10. It spreads slowly by underground rhizomes to form a uniform colony.

directions: Plant in moist to moderately dry, humus-rich soil in full to part shade; jade lily is a close relative of liriope and mondo grass but distinctly more shade-tolerant, native to shaded forest floors of China and Japan and producing neat, arching rosettes of narrow, glossy, dark-green, strap-like leaves that remain evergreen to semi-evergreen through winter in zones 7–10. In autumn it produces short spikes of small, fragrant, pale-pink flowers at ground level, followed by red berries that persist ornamentally into winter. It spreads steadily by short underground rhizomes to form a uniform, weed-suppressing carpet in situations where few other plants will grow — including the dry shade under large trees. More refined and denser in habit than liriope, with a lower, more ground-hugging growth, it is outstanding as a close-knit groundcover for shaded walkways, slopes, and woodland floors. Related to but generally better in deep shade than either liriope or mondo grass.

planting.note: Root hardwood cuttings in late autumn or plant container-grown shrubs in any well-drained soil in full sun to part shade; flowering quince is extremely adaptable, tolerating clay, sandy, poor, and alkaline soils once established. It blooms on old wood — prune only immediately after flowering, never in late summer or fall, or next year's flowers will be removed.

directions: Plant in any well-drained, moderately fertile soil in full sun; flowering quince is one of the earliest-blooming ornamental shrubs of the temperate garden — producing brilliant flowers in red, scarlet, orange, salmon, pink, or white directly on the bare, thorny stems in late winter and very early spring (February–March in the mid-South), often through snow. The dense, interlocking thorny growth makes it an impenetrable barrier hedge as well as an ornamental specimen. After the flowers, the shrub produces hard, green to yellow-green quincelike fruits 2–3 inches across that are not particularly palatable raw but make excellent jelly, preserves, and liqueurs — a quality long exploited in Japanese and Chinese cuisine. The fruits are high in pectin, which makes them particularly valuable for preserving, and have an intense quince fragrance when ripe. Extremely cold-hardy, drought-tolerant once established, and adaptable to a wide range of soils. The many named cultivars span the full color range from pure white ('Jet Trail') to deepest blood-red ('Crimson and Gold').

planting.note: Plant bulbs with the neck at or just above soil level in well-drained, fertile soil in part shade; Scarborough lily blooms best when slightly pot-bound — do not divide unnecessarily. It is semi-evergreen, keeping some leaves through winter, and unlike many South African bulbs does not require a dry rest period. Damaged below 28°F; grow outdoors in zones 9–11 or as a long-lived container plant.

directions: Plant bulbs in well-drained, fertile soil in part shade or as a container specimen; Scarborough lily (formerly Vallota speciosa) is a classic South African bulb in the amaryllis family prized for its outstanding clusters of large, brilliant-red to scarlet, funnel-shaped flowers — 3–4 inches across, borne 3–9 per scape on stout stems rising above arching, strap-like, evergreen leaves in summer (July–September). Unlike most South African bulbs, it does not require a dry rest period and remains semi-evergreen in mild climates, making it simpler to manage than the dormant bulbs. It blooms reliably year after year in the garden in zones 9–11 and is one of the most dependable and longest-lived of all container bulbs in colder climates, where a single large pot can be maintained for decades. The name 'Scarborough Lily' originates from a shipwreck off Scarborough, England in the 1600s — bulbs from the wreck washed ashore and naturalized on the coast, drawing Victorian-era attention to this South African species.

planting.note: Plant bulbs 1–2 inches deep in sharply drained, sandy, slightly acidic to neutral soil in fall; cape cowslip grows through the cool season (October–April) and must be kept completely dry during summer dormancy (May–September) to avoid rot. In zones 9b–11 it can remain in the ground if drainage is perfect; in other climates grow in pots moved to dry storage in summer.

directions: Plant bulbs in fall in sharply drained, sandy, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; Cape cowslip is a small, jewel-like South African bulb of the hyacinth family producing compact clusters of pendent, tubular flowers in brilliant combinations of yellow, orange, red, and green that are uniquely striking even compared to other South African bulbs — the color changes along the length of each tube, with the base often yellow-green and the tips flushed orange-red, creating an overall effect of lit-up coral and gold. The two broad, strap-like leaves are typically spotted or mottled with dark purple, adding to the ornamental interest of the plant even before it flowers in winter and early spring. A winter-growing bulb native to the winter-rainfall Cape region of South Africa, it grows through the cool season (November–April) and enters strict summer dormancy. Outstanding as a cool-season container plant and one of the most popular South African bulbs in the British cut-flower and conservatory tradition for over 200 years.

planting.note: Divide clumps in spring or direct-sow seeds after soil warms in full sun, well-drained, average to lean soil; oriental fountain grass is more cold-hardy than the popular Pennisetum setaceum (tender fountain grass) and survives to zone 6 with good drainage. Cut back to 4–6 inches in late winter before new growth begins.

directions: Plant in well-drained, average to lean soil in full sun; oriental fountain grass is a compact, well-behaved, hardy perennial ornamental grass producing graceful, arching mounds of fine-textured, gray-green foliage topped by exceptionally soft, fluffy flower spikes in shades of soft pink, rose, and cream from June through September — the inflorescences catching the light with a glowing, backlit quality in the evening garden. It is significantly more cold-hardy than the widely grown but tender Pennisetum setaceum (annual fountain grass in zones 6–7) while offering similar mounding, fountain-like habit and soft-colored flower heads. The selection 'Karley Rose' is widely available and offers particularly strong rose-pink coloring on the spikes. It thrives in heat, drought, and poor soil once established and requires only annual cutting back in late winter. An outstanding perennial grass for dry, sunny mixed borders where summer color, fine texture, and movement in the breeze are all desired.
